evaluation_inputs.system_description: A large-scale health care system (millions of lines of code) designed to serve as an information backbone for hospitals, clinics, and HMOs. It consists of two major subsystems: the OnLine Transaction Manager (OLTM) for interactive tasks and the Decision Support and Report Generation Manager (DSRGM) for batch processing. The system handles patient treatment history, insurance tracking, and data warehousing. It is built to be highly configurable to support a product line approach for various regulatory environments and customer needs.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Incomplete and unclear; whole sections were missing, and the architecture was largely presented as inadequately defined box-and-line diagrams.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: Can you verbally fill in the blanks regarding missing architectural sections?
Can you explain the inconsistencies between the layered view and the runtime view (e.g., missing transaction manager and CORBA components)?

hardware_view_description: Different customer offices run different hardware configurations.
The DSRGM subsystem is deployed on UNIX platforms.
The database is hosted on Sun platforms.
runtime_constraints: Must respond faster than the legacy system's 5-second transaction time.
Must support high availability during normal business hours.
Must accommodate high user turnover with ease of learning.

questions: How difficult is it to replace the commercial database with one from another vendor?
How difficult is it to replace the rules engine with compiled C++ code?
How are operating system dependencies encapsulated to allow for OS replacement?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Previously public data is made private, and access is adjusted accordingly.
Data in the information hub is replicated to a branch clinic, and performance is degraded.
A rule in the rule engine fires, and data access is too slow.
A fire in the data center forces the information hub to be moved to a new location.

business_goals: Support the kickoff customer's diverse uses (treatment tracking, payments, trend spotting)
Serve as a basis for an entire product family of health care management systems
Replace multiple legacy systems that are old, difficult to maintain, and unresponsive
Support diverse cultural, regional, language, and currency differences
technical_constraints: Buy rather than build (use off-the-shelf software components)
No lost jobs via retraining of existing employees

architectural_approaches.description: A layered architectural style where the system is organized into hierarchical layers (e.g., presentation, business logic, data access), with significant integration of Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) components to provide standard functionality.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Layers provide separation of concerns, allowing changes to be isolated within a specific layer, reducing impact on other parts of the system.
Use of standardized COTS components can facilitate integration with external systems and data exchange.
Layers can be scaled independently (e.g., adding more application servers to the business logic layer) to handle increased load.

children.refinement: Response time for rule engine data access < 2s
children.scenario_description: A rule in the rule engine fires, and data access must complete within 2 seconds.

children.refinement: System maintains acceptable performance during data replication
children.scenario_description: Data in the information hub is replicated to a branch clinic; system performance (e.g., response time) degrades by no more than 20%.

children.refinement: Change data privacy/access rules within 1 person-day
children.scenario_description: Previously public data is made private, and access rules are adjusted; the change is implemented and deployed within one person-day of effort.

children.refinement: Replace a COTS component in the business logic layer with minimal disruption
children.scenario_description: A new, better COTS component becomes available; the system can integrate it with changes isolated primarily to the business logic layer, causing less than 4 hours of downtime.

children.refinement: Unauthorized access attempts are detected and logged within 5 minutes
children.scenario_description: An external actor attempts to access private patient data; the system detects and logs the attempt within 5 minutes, preventing data breach.

children.refinement: System handles a 50% increase in concurrent users with linear resource scaling
children.scenario_description: User base grows by 50%; the system scales by adding application servers to the business logic layer, maintaining response times within 10% of baseline.

children.refinement: UI adapts to regional language/currency changes with no code deployment
children.scenario_description: The system is deployed in a new region with a different language and currency; administrators configure these via settings, requiring no software deployment or downtime.

children.refinement: Diagnose and resolve performance degradation from replication within 2 hours
children.scenario_description: Performance degrades during data replication; operations team uses system monitoring/logging to identify the bottleneck and resolve it within 2 hours.

children.refinement: Exchange patient data with external regional healthcare system using standard protocols
children.scenario_description: The system must send patient treatment records to an external regional healthcare registry using a standard HL7/FHIR interface, successfully completing the exchange for 99% of records.

scenarios.scenario_text: A rule in the rule engine fires, and data access must complete within 2 seconds.
Data in the information hub is replicated to a branch clinic; system performance (e.g., response time) degrades by no more than 20%.
Previously public data is made private, and access rules are adjusted; the change is implemented and deployed within one person-day of effort.
A new, better COTS component becomes available; the system can integrate it with changes isolated primarily to the business logic layer, causing less than 4 hours of downtime.
An external actor attempts to access private patient data; the system detects and logs the attempt within 5 minutes, preventing data breach.
User base grows by 50%; the system scales by adding application servers to the business logic layer, maintaining response times within 10% of baseline.
The system is deployed in a new region with a different language and currency; administrators configure these via settings, requiring no software deployment or downtime.
Performance degrades during data replication; operations team uses system monitoring/logging to identify the bottleneck and resolve it within 2 hours.
The system must send patient treatment records to an external regional healthcare registry using a standard HL7/FHIR interface, successfully completing the exchange for 99% of records.

scenario_reference.text: A rule in the rule engine fires, and data access must complete within 2 seconds.

analysis_records.stimulus: Rule engine fires, triggering data access request.
architectural_decisions: Use of a layered architecture separating rule engine, business logic, and data access.
Integration of COTS rule engine and database components.
Implementation of caching at the business logic or data access layer.
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered architecture introduces network hops between components, which can increase latency. COTS components may have non-optimized data access paths. Caching can mitigate latency for frequently accessed data but adds complexity.

risks.description: Network latency between the rule engine layer and the data source layer may cause the 2-second threshold to be exceeded, especially if data is not co-located.
risks.rationale: The layered architecture inherently adds communication overhead. Without low-latency network links and efficient query design, response time may suffer.

risks.description: The COTS rule engine or database may have performance limitations under high load, causing slow data retrieval.
risks.rationale: COTS components are generic and may not be optimized for the specific data access patterns of the rule engine, leading to unpredictable performance.

non_risks.description: Separation of concerns via layers localizes performance tuning to the data access layer.
non_risks.rationale: The layered style allows optimization of the data access layer (e.g., query optimization, indexing) without affecting the rule engine logic, reducing risk of widespread changes.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Network round-trip time between application server and database

scenario_reference.text: Data in the information hub is replicated to a branch clinic; system performance (e.g., response time) degrades by no more than 20%.

analysis_records.stimulus: Initiation of data replication process from information hub to branch clinic.
analysis_records.response_measure: System performance degradation <= 20% of baseline response time.
architectural_decisions: Use of asynchronous replication to decouple primary operations from replication tasks.
Allocation of separate network bandwidth or dedicated replication servers.
Layered isolation where replication logic resides in a separate service layer.
analysis_records.reasoning: Asynchronous replication reduces immediate impact on user transactions but may consume shared resources (CPU, I/O, network). Dedicated resources help but increase cost. Layered isolation can contain the performance impact to specific layers.

risks.description: Replication process consumes significant I/O bandwidth on the shared database server, causing contention and slowing down primary user transactions.
risks.rationale: If replication is not properly throttled or uses the same data storage layer, it can directly compete with operational data access, leading to >20% degradation.

non_risks.description: Asynchronous replication design prevents replication latency from blocking user requests.
non_risks.rationale: By decoupling the replication flow from the main transaction path, the user-facing layers can maintain responsiveness even if replication lags.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Available I/O throughput on the database server

conflicting_attributes: Performance (Async reduces impact on primary operations)
Data Consistency (Async may lead to temporary data staleness)

scenario_reference.text: Previously public data is made private, and access rules are adjusted; the change is implemented and deployed within one person-day of effort.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations, during a change window
analysis_records.stimulus: Business requirement change to data privacy/access rules.
analysis_records.response_measure: Change implemented and deployed within 1 person-day.
architectural_decisions: Centralized access control logic within a dedicated security/authorization layer.
Use of declarative, configuration-based rule definitions (potentially via a COTS security component).
Layered separation ensuring data privacy changes are confined to the business logic and security layers.
analysis_records.reasoning: A centralized, configurable security layer allows rule changes without code modifications. The layered architecture isolates changes, preventing ripple effects to presentation or data layers. COTS security components may offer rapid reconfiguration.

risks.description: Access rules are hard-coded or scattered across multiple application modules, requiring extensive code changes and testing.
risks.rationale: If the architecture does not centralize security policy, a simple rule change could necessitate modifications in many places, exceeding the 1-day effort limit.

non_risks.description: Layered architecture with a dedicated security service localizes the change impact.
non_risks.rationale: The change is confined to the security layer's configuration or service logic, minimizing testing and deployment scope.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Granularity and complexity of the access control policy model

conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (improved, as changes are in one place)
Performance (may add latency due to extra authorization calls)

scenario_reference.text: A new, better COTS component becomes available; the system can integrate it with changes isolated primarily to the business logic layer, causing less than 4 hours of downtime.

analysis_records.stimulus: Availability of a new, superior COTS component for integration.
analysis_records.response_measure: Integration completed with changes primarily in business logic layer and < 4 hours downtime.
architectural_decisions: Strict layer encapsulation with well-defined interfaces between business logic and COTS components.
Use of adapter/wrapper patterns to abstract COTS-specific APIs.
Dependency injection or service locator patterns to allow runtime component swapping where possible.
analysis_records.reasoning: Well-defined interfaces and abstraction patterns minimize the impact of swapping a COTS component. However, if the new component's interface or semantics differ significantly, the adapter layer may require substantial changes, potentially affecting other layers. Downtime is primarily for deployment and data migration.

risks.description: Tight coupling between business logic and the specific COTS component's proprietary API, requiring extensive rewrites beyond the business logic layer.
risks.rationale: Without a clean abstraction layer, replacing the COTS component may necessitate changes in data models, presentation logic, or even other integrated systems, increasing downtime and effort.

non_risks.description: Use of adapter pattern and dependency injection confines integration changes to the component wrapper and configuration.
non_risks.rationale: The core business logic interacts with a stable interface; only the adapter implementing that interface for the new COTS needs to be changed, limiting scope.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Stability and completeness of the abstract interface definition for the COTS functionality

tradeoff_points.parameter: Level of abstraction over COTS components

scenario_reference.text: An external actor attempts to access private patient data; the system detects and logs the attempt within 5 minutes, preventing data breach.

analysis_records.stimulus: Unauthorized access attempt to private patient data.
analysis_records.response_measure: Attempt detected and logged within 5 minutes; data breach prevented.
architectural_decisions: Implementation of a centralized audit logging service (potentially a COTS SIEM or logging component).
Integration of intrusion detection mechanisms at network and application layers.
Strict access control enforcement at each layer (presentation, business logic, data).
analysis_records.reasoning: Centralized logging enables correlation and timely detection. Layered security provides defense in depth. However, detection latency depends on log aggregation speed and analysis rule efficiency. COTS security components may offer out-of-the-box detection but require proper configuration.

risks.description: Logs are generated but not monitored in real-time; detection relies on manual review, causing delays beyond 5 minutes.
risks.rationale: Without automated log analysis and alerting, even a well-logged event may not be noticed promptly, failing the detection time requirement.

non_risks.description: Layered architecture allows access control checks at multiple points (e.g., API gateway, business service, database), increasing chances of blocking unauthorized access early.
non_risks.rationale: An attempt blocked at the presentation layer gateway may never reach business logic or data, simplifying detection and prevention.

tradeoff_points.parameter: Granularity and volume of security logging

scenario_reference.text: User base grows by 50%; the system scales by adding application servers to the business logic layer, maintaining response times within 10% of baseline.

analysis_records.response_measure: Response times maintained within 10% of baseline via horizontal scaling of business logic layer.
architectural_decisions: Stateless design of business logic components to enable horizontal scaling.
Use of a load balancer in front of the business logic layer.
Layered separation allowing independent scaling of the business logic tier.
analysis_records.reasoning: Statelessness is key for linear scaling. The load balancer distributes traffic. The layered architecture allows scaling the business logic layer without necessarily scaling presentation or data layers. However, the shared data layer (database) may become a bottleneck.

risks.description: The database layer becomes a bottleneck under increased load, preventing the business logic layer from maintaining response times despite scaling.
risks.rationale: Scaling the application servers increases database connection pools and query load. If the database is not also scaled or optimized, response times will degrade beyond 10%.

non_risks.description: Stateless business logic components allow new application servers to be added seamlessly to the load-balanced pool.
non_risks.rationale: No user session affinity is required, so traffic distribution is efficient, supporting linear scaling for the business logic layer itself.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Database connection pool size and query throughput

scenario_reference.text: The system is deployed in a new region with a different language and currency; administrators configure these via settings, requiring no software deployment or downtime.

analysis_records.environment: System expansion to a new geographical region
analysis_records.stimulus: Need to support new regional settings (language, currency).
analysis_records.response_measure: Configuration via admin settings, zero deployment/downtime.
architectural_decisions: Externalization of all locale-specific strings and formats (like currency symbols) to resource files or a configuration database.
Presentation layer designed to dynamically load locale resources based on user or system settings.
Use of COTS localization libraries or frameworks.
analysis_records.reasoning: Externalizing configuration separates regional concerns from code. The layered architecture allows the presentation layer to adapt without changes to business logic or data layers. COTS libraries may simplify implementation but must support dynamic reconfiguration.

risks.description: Locale-specific logic (e.g., date formatting, currency calculations) is embedded in business logic code, requiring code changes and redeployment.
risks.rationale: If internationalization is not fully externalized, a new region may require modifications to core business rules, violating the 'no deployment' requirement.

non_risks.description: Layered architecture confines localization changes to the presentation layer and external resource files.
non_risks.rationale: Business logic and data layers remain unchanged; only configuration files for the new locale need to be added and activated by administrators.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Completeness of internationalization (i18n) coverage in the codebase

tradeoff_points.parameter: Dynamic vs. static locale resource loading
conflicting_attributes: Performance (May increase initial page load time)

scenario_reference.text: Performance degrades during data replication; operations team uses system monitoring/logging to identify the bottleneck and resolve it within 2 hours.

analysis_records.stimulus: Performance degradation observed during data replication.
analysis_records.response_measure: Bottleneck identified and resolved within 2 hours using monitoring/logging.
architectural_decisions: Comprehensive, centralized logging and metrics collection across all layers.
Use of COTS application performance monitoring (APM) and log aggregation tools.
Layered instrumentation to isolate metrics per component (e.g., database I/O, network latency, business logic execution time).
analysis_records.reasoning: Centralized monitoring with layer-specific metrics enables rapid root cause isolation. COTS APM tools can provide out-of-the-box dashboards and alerts. However, if logging is insufficient or metrics are too coarse, diagnosis may take longer than 2 hours.

risks.description: Monitoring lacks granular metrics for the replication process itself (e.g., replication queue length, network throughput to branch), delaying identification of the specific bottleneck.
risks.rationale: Without detailed metrics for the replication subsystem, operations must rely on generic system metrics (CPU, memory), which may not pinpoint the cause, extending diagnosis time.

non_risks.description: Layered architecture allows monitoring agents to be deployed per layer, providing clear segmentation of performance data.
non_risks.rationale: Operations can quickly determine if degradation is in the data layer (high I/O), network layer (high latency), or application layer (high CPU), narrowing the search.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Alert threshold sensitivity and correlation rules in the monitoring system

scenario_reference.text: The system must send patient treatment records to an external regional healthcare registry using a standard HL7/FHIR interface, successfully completing the exchange for 99% of records.

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations, during data exchange batches
analysis_records.stimulus: Requirement to send patient treatment records to an external registry.
analysis_records.response_measure: 99% successful exchange completion using standard HL7/FHIR interface.
architectural_decisions: Implementation of a dedicated integration layer (e.g., Enterprise Service Bus - ESB) or adapter for protocol translation.
Use of COTS healthcare integration engines supporting HL7/FHIR standards.
Layered isolation where integration logic is separate from core business logic.
analysis_records.reasoning: A dedicated integration layer handles protocol specifics, transformation, and error handling. COTS integration engines reduce custom code. The layered style prevents interoperability concerns from polluting core business logic. Success rate depends on network reliability, data quality, and external system availability.

risks.description: External registry's HL7/FHIR implementation may have non-standard extensions or validation rules, causing message rejections and lowering success rate below 99%.
risks.rationale: Even with standards, interoperability often requires custom mapping and testing. Without a flexible adapter, message failures may be frequent.

non_risks.description: Use of a COTS integration engine with built-in HL7/FHIR connectors reduces development effort and leverages proven interoperability features.
non_risks.rationale: COTS components are often certified for standards compliance and include error handling and retry mechanisms, increasing likelihood of successful exchange.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Network reliability and latency between the system and the external registry

tradeoff_points.parameter: Coupling between business logic and external interface
conflicting_attributes: Interoperability (Loose coupling via integration layer improves adaptability)
Performance (Additional hop through integration layer may add latency)
